evals.name: observable-viewmodel-simple
evals.expected_output: @StateObject is the Combine/ObservableObject property wrapper — it wraps the value in a Combine subscriber. @Observable uses Swift's Observation framework, not Combine. Using @StateObject with @Observable compiles but will not observe property changes — the view won't update when posts changes. Fix: use `@State private var viewModel = FeedViewModel()`. @State is the correct wrapper for @Observable classes owned by a view.

assertions.text: Explains the view won't update because @StateObject uses Combine, not Observation
assertions.rationale: Skill explains the mechanism: @StateObject wraps a Combine subscriber, so Observation property changes are not detected — baseline often just says 'use @State' without explaining why

assertions.text: Fixes with @State private var viewModel = FeedViewModel()
assertions.rationale: Correct wrapper from skill's ownership decision tree

evals.name: observable-viewmodel-medium
evals.expected_output: @ObservedObject is for ObservableObject — wrong for @Observable. For @Observable ViewModels passed from a parent that need $ bindings (like TextField's text parameter), use @Bindable: `@Bindable var viewModel: FormViewModel`. This lets you write `$viewModel.name` to create a binding to the name property. If no $ bindings are needed (read-only), use a plain `let viewModel: FormViewModel`.

assertions.text: Identifies @ObservedObject as wrong for @Observable (it's for ObservableObject)
assertions.rationale: Common migration mistake from the Combine world

assertions.text: Recommends @Bindable for @Observable ViewModels that need $ bindings
assertions.rationale: @Bindable is the @Observable-specific wrapper for two-way bindings — baseline often doesn't know this and suggests @ObservedObject or @EnvironmentObject

assertions.text: Notes plain let viewModel is sufficient when only reading (no $ bindings needed)
assertions.rationale: Skill's ownership decision tree: @Bindable only when $ bindings are needed — avoids unnecessary complexity

evals.name: observable-viewmodel-complex
evals.expected_output: Three violations: (1) SwiftUI import — ViewModel must import only Foundation. Color is a SwiftUI type; expose a semantic string instead (`headerColorName: String = "blue"`) and let the View convert it. This makes the ViewModel testable without a SwiftUI environment. (2) Missing @MainActor — state mutations in load() happen on the caller's isolation; without @MainActor, they may run on a background thread, causing runtime warnings and potential view update issues in iOS 17+. (3) Missing final and @MainActor: the full declaration must be `@Observable @MainActor final class DashboardViewModel`.

assertions.text: Identifies SwiftUI import as a violation — ViewModel must import only Foundation
assertions.rationale: SwiftUI import in VM breaks unit testability — skill rule mirroring the UIKit version but for SwiftUI

assertions.text: Identifies missing @MainActor — state mutations from async load() may run off main thread
assertions.rationale: @MainActor is required on all ViewModels per skill rule — baseline often omits this; iOS 17 warns without it

assertions.text: Provides corrected declaration: @Observable @MainActor final class DashboardViewModel
assertions.rationale: All three modifiers are required together per skill's code generation rules

assertions.text: Corrected code adds private(set) on state property and CancellationError silent handling in load()
assertions.rationale: WITH response adds private(set) var state to prevent external mutation and catch is CancellationError { return } in corrected code — WITHOUT omits private(set) and only adds CancellationError as an afterthought

assertions.text: Corrected code injects repository via constructor (init(repository: ItemRepositoryProtocol)) instead of using undeclared property
assertions.rationale: WITH applies constructor injection pattern to the corrected code — WITHOUT leaves repository as an undeclared implicit dependency

evals.name: task-lifecycle-simple
evals.expected_output: Two issues with onAppear { Task { } }: (1) The Task is unmanaged — it is not cancelled when the view disappears (e.g., user navigates away mid-load). The network request continues in the background, and when it completes it may update a deallocated ViewModel. (2) onAppear fires every time the view appears — navigating away and back triggers a duplicate load. Fix: use `.task { await viewModel.loadProducts() }`. SwiftUI automatically cancels the Task when the view disappears and runs it once on initial appearance.

assertions.text: Identifies Task in onAppear is unmanaged — not cancelled when view disappears
assertions.rationale: Core .task vs onAppear anti-pattern from skill

assertions.text: Fixes with .task { await viewModel.loadProducts() }
assertions.rationale: Skill's prescribed fix: .task manages lifecycle, auto-cancels on disappear

evals.name: task-lifecycle-medium

assertions.text: Adds catch is CancellationError { return } before the generic catch
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: CancellationError must be caught separately and return silently — baseline often says 'ignore errors' without the specific pattern

evals.name: task-lifecycle-complex
evals.expected_output: .task without an id parameter runs once on initial appearance and is not re-triggered when selectedFilter changes. The view updates the picker (because @Observable tracks selectedFilter), but the .task doesn't re-run to reload data. Fix: add the filter as the task's id: `.task(id: viewModel.selectedFilter) { await viewModel.loadFeed() }`. SwiftUI automatically cancels the current task and starts a new one whenever the id value changes.

assertions.text: Identifies .task without id does not re-run when selectedFilter changes
assertions.rationale: .task(id:) for reactive reloads is a key SwiftUI pattern from the skill — baseline often misses it and suggests manual Task creation instead

assertions.text: Fixes with .task(id: viewModel.selectedFilter) { await viewModel.loadFeed() }
assertions.rationale: Exact .task(id:) API is the skill's prescribed solution — auto-cancels previous task on id change

assertions.text: Explains SwiftUI cancels the current task and starts a new one on id change
assertions.rationale: Mechanical explanation of why .task(id:) solves the reactivity problem

evals.name: viewstate-simple
evals.expected_output: Separate boolean flags allow impossible states: isLoading = true AND errorMessage != nil simultaneously; isEmpty = true AND articles.count > 0; all four properties could be true at once. The View must guard against every combination. Fix: replace all four with a single `ViewState<[Article]>` enum: `.loading`, `.loaded([Article])`, `.empty`, `.error(String)`. These are mutually exclusive by definition — one property, four possible states, zero impossible combinations.

assertions.text: Recommends ViewState<T> enum with mutually exclusive cases (.loading, .loaded, .empty, .error)
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes ViewState<T> specifically — baseline may suggest combining properties rather than using an enum

evals.name: viewstate-medium
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) Using == comparison on ViewState requires Equatable conformance, and casting with as? is fragile and verbose. Prefer an exhaustive switch or if case pattern matching: `switch viewModel.state { case .loading: ProgressView() case .loaded(let articles): List(...) case .error(let msg): Text(msg) case .empty: ContentUnavailableView(...) }`. (2) The .empty and .error cases are not handled — the view renders nothing for these states, showing a blank screen with no feedback to the user.

assertions.text: Recommends switch or if case pattern matching over == comparison and casting
assertions.rationale: Pattern matching is the correct approach for enum associated values — baseline often uses == which requires Equatable

evals.name: viewstate-complex
evals.expected_output: Three issues: (1) Network call directly in ViewModel — violates Repository pattern. Networking must go through an injected protocol (`ProductRepositoryProtocol`) for testability. The ViewModel should call `try await repository.fetchProducts()`, not construct URLSession calls. (2) Force-try on both network and decode — any failure (network error, malformed JSON) crashes the app. Wrap in do-catch: set state = .error(error.localizedDescription) in the catch. (3) No CancellationError guard — when .task cancels this function, the force-try! on URLSession throws CancellationError, crashing. Add `catch is CancellationError { return }` before the generic catch.

assertions.text: Identifies direct URLSession call in ViewModel — must go through injected Repository protocol
assertions.rationale: Repository pattern from networking.md — ViewModel never constructs network URLs directly

assertions.text: Identifies force-try! crashes on network/decode errors — wrap in do-catch with .error state
assertions.rationale: Error handling must use ViewState.error, not crash

assertions.text: Identifies missing CancellationError guard — task cancellation causes force-try crash
assertions.rationale: Interaction of force-try and CancellationError is a compound issue from skill — baseline often misses the cancellation angle

evals.name: navigation-simple
evals.expected_output:
String-based routing is an anti-pattern: (1) Typos cause silent failures — appending "product-detial" shows nothing, no compiler error. (2) Not inspectable or serializable for state restoration. (3) Breaks as the app grows — matching strings across files is fragile. Fix: define a typed enum:

```swift
enum Route: Hashable {
    case productDetail(productID: UUID)
    case settings
}
```

Then: `path.append(Route.productDetail(productID: id))` and `.navigationDestination(for: Route.self) { route in switch route { ... } }`.

assertions.text: Recommends enum Route: Hashable with typed cases
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes typed enum with Hashable — baseline may suggest any navigation improvement without the enum/Hashable specifics

assertions.text: Notes compile-time safety and state restoration as benefits of typed routes
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific benefits of typed routing

evals.name: navigation-medium
evals.expected_output: The outer NavigationStack wrapping the TabView is wrong. A NavigationStack must not wrap a TabView — this causes: duplicate toolbars, broken back button behavior, and unpredictable navigation state. Each tab should have its own independent NavigationStack, which the inner stacks already provide correctly. Fix: remove the outer NavigationStack. The correct structure: TabView at the root, each tab contains its own NavigationStack.

assertions.text: Identifies the outer NavigationStack wrapping TabView as the violation
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly names this as a critical anti-pattern — NavigationStack inside NavigationStack or wrapping TabView causes broken UI

assertions.text: Confirms inner per-tab NavigationStacks are correct — each tab independently manages its stack
assertions.rationale: Correct TabView + NavigationStack structure from navigation.md

assertions.text: Notes duplicate toolbars and broken back buttons as the symptoms
assertions.rationale: Concrete symptoms from the skill's anti-patterns section

assertions.text: Shows a TabRouter @Observable class pattern with typed per-tab route arrays (e.g., homeRoutes: [HomeRoute], profileRoutes: [ProfileRoute]) and NavigationStack(path:) binding
assertions.rationale: WITH response provides a production TabRouter @Observable with typed NavigationStack paths per tab — WITHOUT only shows the basic fix without a Router architecture

evals.name: navigation-complex
evals.expected_output: Navigation state (showProfile, showSettings, selectedItemID) belongs in the View layer, not the ViewModel. ViewModels should not manage presentation state — they can't be unit tested for it meaningfully, and it couples navigation to business logic. Two patterns: (1) For sheet/full-screen presentation: move boolean flags to the View as @State, ViewModel signals intent via closures (`var onOpenProfile: (() -> Void)?`), View sets the closure to toggle its own @State. (2) For NavigationStack: use a Router @Observable class at the view level holding a NavigationPath, or use typed Route enum with .navigationDestination. Either way, ViewModel signals 'navigate to X', View/Router decides how.

assertions.text: Identifies navigation booleans in ViewModel are a violation — navigation state belongs in the View layer
assertions.rationale: ViewModel should not own presentation state — skill-specific guidance from navigation.md

assertions.text: Recommends ViewModel signals intent via closures (onOpenProfile?), View toggles its own @State
assertions.rationale: Navigation closure pattern mirrors UIKit MVVM coordinator pattern — baseline often leaves navigation in ViewModel

assertions.text: Mentions Router @Observable or NavigationPath at the View level as an alternative
assertions.rationale: Router pattern from navigation.md for programmatic navigation

evals.name: dependency-injection-simple
evals.expected_output: APIService.shared is a singleton — a hidden dependency the ViewModel creates internally. This makes CartViewModel untestable: you can't inject a mock to control responses. Fix: inject via constructor with a protocol type: `init(service: CartServiceProtocol)`. The View reads the service from @Environment and passes it to the ViewModel on init. In tests, pass a MockCartService with stubbed responses.

assertions.text: Recommends constructor injection with a protocol type: init(service: CartServiceProtocol)
assertions.rationale: Protocol-based injection enables mock substitution in tests

assertions.text: Shows @Entry-based EnvironmentValues registration for the service (extension EnvironmentValues { @Entry var cartService: CartServiceProtocol = LiveCartService() })
assertions.rationale: WITH response shows the full @Entry pattern for custom environment keys (iOS 17+) — WITHOUT only mentions @Environment in passing without the @Entry registration

assertions.text: Marks the service protocol as Sendable (protocol CartServiceProtocol: Sendable)
assertions.rationale: WITH adds Sendable conformance for thread-safe cross-actor passing — WITHOUT omits Sendable on the protocol definition

evals.name: dependency-injection-medium
evals.expected_output: @EnvironmentObject and .environmentObject(_:) are for ObservableObject (Combine). For @Observable types, use the Observation-native environment APIs: inject with `.environment(AppTheme())` and read with `@Environment(AppTheme.self) var theme`. Using @EnvironmentObject with @Observable either crashes at runtime (missing environmentObject) or silently fails to observe changes.

assertions.text: Identifies .environmentObject and @EnvironmentObject as wrong for @Observable
assertions.rationale: @EnvironmentObject is Combine-only — this is the most common @Observable DI mistake, explicitly covered in dependency-injection.md

assertions.text: Fixes injection with .environment(AppTheme()) and reading with @Environment(AppTheme.self)
assertions.rationale: @Environment(Type.self) is the @Observable-native pattern — baseline often doesn't know this API

assertions.text: Notes mixing @EnvironmentObject with @Observable can crash at runtime or silently not observe
assertions.rationale: Explains the failure mode of using the wrong wrapper

assertions.text: Warns against passing @Observable instance inline to .environment() without a stable @State owner — object re-created every render
assertions.rationale: WITH explicitly warns 'Never pass AppTheme() inline to .environment() without a stable owner' and shows @State ownership in App struct — WITHOUT does not warn about this re-creation pitfall

evals.name: dependency-injection-complex

assertions.text: Uses @Entry to define a custom EnvironmentValues key with a default value
assertions.rationale: @Entry is the iOS 17+ SwiftUI API for custom environment values — skill-specific knowledge baseline rarely knows

assertions.text: Uses .environment(\.networkService, ...) and @Environment(\.networkService) for injection/reading
assertions.rationale: Correct environment key-path based pattern for protocol types

assertions.text: Shows test override via .environment(\.networkService, MockNetworkService()) without changing ViewModel
assertions.rationale: Test DI via environment override is the key testability benefit — skill documents this as the recommended approach

evals.name: testing-simple

assertions.text: Fixes by marking the test class @MainActor
assertions.rationale: Class-level @MainActor is the skill's prescribed approach — more ergonomic than marking each method

evals.name: testing-medium

assertions.text: Identifies missing memory leak detection — addTeardownBlock { [weak sut] in XCTAssertNil(sut) }
assertions.rationale: addTeardownBlock leak pattern is explicitly required in skill's test generation rules

assertions.text: Shows direct await + assert pattern without XCTestExpectation for straightforward async tests
assertions.rationale: Skill notes XCTestExpectation is only for async callbacks — direct await is simpler and correct here

assertions.text: Uses if case pattern matching to unwrap .loaded state in assertion
assertions.rationale: Correct way to assert on enum with associated value

evals.name: testing-complex

assertions.text: Includes addTeardownBlock { [weak sut] in XCTAssertNil(sut) } for memory leak detection
assertions.rationale: Required in all test setups per skill's test generation rules

assertions.text: Mock uses var searchCallCount = 0 and var shouldThrowCancellation to track calls and control behavior
assertions.rationale: Skill's mock pattern: stubbed* for return values, *CallCount for tracking — baseline often uses more complex spy patterns

evals.name: performance-medium
evals.expected_output: With @Observable, each view tracks only the properties it actually reads. FeedView reads viewModel.posts and viewModel.isRefreshing — it should NOT redraw when searchQuery changes. However, if HomeViewModel is a single large class and FeedView's @Bindable captures the whole object, SwiftUI may track the entire object scope. The fix is to verify FeedView doesn't access searchQuery at all (check body for any hidden reads). If it still redraws, split HomeViewModel: FeedViewModel (posts, isRefreshing) and SearchViewModel (searchQuery) — separate @Observable classes so they track independently. Pass each subViewModel to the relevant view.

assertions.text: Explains @Observable tracks only properties actually read by the view body
assertions.rationale: Granular observation is @Observable's key advantage over ObservableObject — skill documents this explicitly

assertions.text: Recommends splitting into focused ViewModels (FeedViewModel, SearchViewModel) as the architectural fix
assertions.rationale: Splitting @Observable classes for independent tracking is the skill's prescribed solution from performance.md

assertions.text: Suggests verifying FeedView body has no hidden reads of searchQuery
assertions.rationale: Hidden reads (e.g., through computed properties) can cause unexpected tracking

assertions.text: Provides a verification step: add Self._printChanges() to both views after splitting, confirm only SearchBar prints during typing, then remove before committing
assertions.rationale: WITH includes an explicit post-fix verification workflow using Self._printChanges() on both views — WITHOUT mentions the tool but does not prescribe a structured verification procedure

evals.name: performance-complex
evals.expected_output: Two distinct problems: (1) God ViewModel anti-pattern — one @Observable class with 15+ unrelated properties. Any property change (searchQuery, theme, notifications) redraws ALL views that hold this type, even if they only read `feed`. @Observable's granular tracking only helps when a view reads a subset of one instance's properties — a 20-property class still creates broad tracking surface. Fix: split into focused ViewModels: FeedViewModel, ProfileViewModel, CartViewModel, each with 3-5 focused properties. (2) Each view creates its own AppViewModel instance with @State — they don't share state at all. ProfileView's `vm.profile` and FeedView's `vm.feed` are completely separate objects. This is likely a bug. Shared state must be injected from a common ancestor or via @Environment.

assertions.text: Identifies God ViewModel anti-pattern — too many unrelated properties cause broad redraws
assertions.rationale: Skill recommends focused ViewModels (3-5 properties) as a performance pattern from performance.md

assertions.text: Identifies each view creates a separate @State AppViewModel instance — no shared state
assertions.rationale: @State creates a new instance per view — a common misconception that @State is shared; only shared via @Environment or passed references

assertions.text: Recommends splitting into focused ViewModels and injecting shared state via @Environment
assertions.rationale: Correct fix addressing both the redraw and the shared-state bug

evals.name: anti-patterns-simple
evals.expected_output: 🔴 Critical: @StateObject with an @Observable class. @StateObject is for ObservableObject (Combine-based). Using it with @Observable causes the view to NOT update when count changes — @StateObject uses Combine's objectWillChange publisher, which @Observable doesn't publish to. The button label will stay at '0' forever. Fix immediately: `@State private var viewModel = CounterViewModel()`.

assertions.text: Classifies @StateObject with @Observable with explicit severity level (e.g., critical/high/medium)
assertions.rationale: Skill ranks this as the top violation — view silently stops updating, hard to diagnose

assertions.text: Explains the view will not update because @StateObject uses Combine, not Observation
assertions.rationale: Mechanical explanation of why the UI breaks

assertions.text: Fixes with @State private var viewModel = CounterViewModel()
assertions.rationale: Correct wrapper for @Observable owned by a view

assertions.text: Provides a complete migration mapping table showing all three wrong/correct wrapper pairs: @StateObject->@State, @ObservedObject->let/@Bindable, @EnvironmentObject->@Environment(Type.self)
assertions.rationale: WITH includes a full detection rule table mapping all Combine wrappers to their @Observable equivalents — WITHOUT only covers the single @StateObject->@State pair in context

evals.name: anti-patterns-medium
evals.expected_output: Four violations ranked: (1) 🔴 Critical: @StateObject with @Observable — view won't observe ViewModel changes. Fix: @State private var viewModel. (2) 🟡 High: onAppear { Task { } } instead of .task — unmanaged task, not cancelled on disappear, fires on every appearance. Fix: .task { await viewModel.loadCart() }. (3) 🟡 High: Task {} in button action — acceptable for one-off user-initiated actions (the task is short-lived), but should ensure CancellationError is handled inside processPayment(). This is a borderline case per the skill. (4) 🟢 Medium: Separate isLoading/cart/error booleans instead of ViewState<Cart> enum — allows impossible states.

assertions.text: Classifies @StateObject with @Observable with explicit severity level as the most severe violation
assertions.rationale: Severity ranking from anti-patterns.md — this is always the top violation

assertions.text: Flags onAppear + Task as High — unmanaged, fires on every appearance
assertions.rationale: Core task lifecycle anti-pattern

assertions.text: Flags separate boolean flags (isLoading/cart/error) as Medium — use ViewState<Cart>
assertions.rationale: Impossible state pattern is Medium severity — less urgent than the breaking Critical

evals.name: anti-patterns-complex
evals.expected_output: Six violations ranked: (1) 🔴 Critical: SwiftUI import in ViewModel — breaks unit testability. (2) 🔴 Critical: UIViewController reference in ViewModel — UIKit type in SwiftUI ViewModel, retain cycle risk, navigation in ViewModel. (3) 🔴 Critical: Missing @MainActor — async search() mutates state on caller's thread, not guaranteed main. (4) 🟡 High: viewModel without @State — SwiftUI creates a new SearchViewModel on every redraw; search state resets constantly. Fix: @State private var viewModel. (5) 🟡 High: onAppear { Task {} } instead of .task — unmanaged task. (6) 🟢 Medium: force-try! on network call crashes on any error; separate boolean flags instead of ViewState. Also: APIService.shared singleton prevents testing.

assertions.text: Flags SwiftUI import, UIViewController reference, and missing @MainActor as Critical
assertions.rationale: All three are Critical per anti-patterns.md — baseline often misses the @MainActor omission as Critical

assertions.text: Identifies viewModel without @State as High — new instance created on every redraw
assertions.rationale: Missing @State causes ViewModel recreation on redraw — SwiftUI-specific issue baseline often overlooks

assertions.text: Ranks violations by severity with critical issues listed before lower-severity ones
assertions.rationale: Severity ranking discipline from anti-patterns.md — baseline often treats all violations equally

methodology: v3: removed 100%-pass non-discriminating assertions, softened taxonomy references to test behavior not labels, removed library-name-dropping and impractical assertions, removed difficulty and discriminating fields
